California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
January 13, 1989 
Victor J. James, II 
Littler, Mendelson, Fastiff & Tichy 
455 Capitol Mall, suite 420 
Sacramento, CA 
95814-4405 
Dear Mr. James: 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-88-469 
You have requested advice on behalf of board members Paul 
E. Stefani, Milo Wetzel and Rosemarie Butler of the River Delta 
Unified School District Board of Trustees concerning their 
responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").Y 
This letter addresses only the duties of the school board 
members under the Act. 
There may be other restrictions imposed 
by other statutes, such as Government Code section 1090. 
The 
district attorney and the Attorney General are responsible for 
enforcing Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
The spouses of board members Paul E. Stefani, Milo Wetzel 
and Rosemarie Butler are employed by the River Delta Unified 
School District (the "District") and are members of the 
Y 
Government Code Sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
indicated. 
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code 
of Regulations section 18000, et seq. 
All references to 
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code 
of Regulations. 
"8 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 


Victor J. James II 
January 13, 1989 
Page 2 
certificated bargaining unit for collective bargaining 
purposes. 
May the board members participate in a decision to 
ratify a collective bargaining agreement affecting the 
certificated employees including their spouses? 
CONCLUSION 
Under the Act, the board members may participate in the 
decision to ratify the collective bargaining agreement 
affecting the certificated employees including their spouses. 
FACTS 
You have indicated that you understand, based on section 
82030(b) (2) and Regulation 18702.1(c) (2) (now renumbered as 
Regulation 18702.1(c) (1» 
that a board member does not have to 
disqualify himself or herself with regard to a decision solely 
related to a spouse's salary, per diem, or reimbursement for 
spouses from the District. 
You are concerned, however, since 
the collective bargaining agreement for school district 
employees covers many aspects of compensation. 
In addition to 
salary, the agreement includes health benefits, deferred 
compensation, tax sheltered annuities, life insurance, leaves, 
etc. 
ANALYSIS 
In the past the commission has held that pension benefits 
received from a county are not income within the meaning of 
Section 82030(a). 
In its opinion, the Commission noted: 
When an employee agrees to work for the county, 
he is entitled to certain benefits which comprise 
his compensation. 
He receives, for example, a 
monthly salary, vacation and sick leave and 
retirement benefits .... 
[A]s the California 
Supreme Court has observed, retirement benefits, 
such as pension, "do not represent the 
beneficient (sic) gratuities of the employer; 
they are, rather, part of the consideration 
earned by the employee." 
In other wordS, the 
pension payment serves as a renumeration for 
services rendered by the employee .... " 
A 
pension, therefore, is essentially a type-of 
deferred salary whereby an employee agrees to 
receive a smaller payment while working in 
exchange for the security of receiving the 
remaining portion of his compensation after he 
retires. 
In re Moore, (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 33, 36 
(citations and footnote omitted, 
emphasis added). 


Victor J. James II 
January 13, 1989 
Page 3 
The analysis in Moore, supra, is applicable to other fringe 
benefits as well such as health benefits, deferred 
compensation, tax sheltered annuities, life insurance, leaves, 
etc. 
These fringe benefits are part of salary in that the 
employee agrees to receive a smaller payment in exchange for 
receiving the remaining portion of his compensation by way of 
these fringe benefits. 
Thus, if such fringe benefits are part 
of a compensation package paid by a government agency, they are 
included within the term salary in section 82030(b) (2). 
Accordingly, the three members of the River Delta Unified 
School District Board of Trustees may participate in the 
decision to ratify a collective bargaining agreement affecting 
the certificated employees including their spouses. 
However, 
they may not participate in decisions to hire, fire, promote, 
demote, or discipline an official's spouse, or to set a salary 
for an official's spouse which is different from salaries paid 
to other employees of the spouse's agency in the same job 
classification or position. 
(Regulation 18702.1(c) (1), copy 
enclosed.) 
See also Saint-Evens Advice Letter, No. A-83-172, 
and Flitner Advice Letter, No. A-87-096 (copies enclosed). 
Although the Act may not require disqualification from a 
particular decision, other statutory provisions, such as 
Government Code section 1090, may restrict the board members 
ability to participate in the decision. 
The Commission, 
however, does not administer this section. 
We are, therefore, 
unable to give you any advice regarding Section 1090. 
If you have any further questions regarding this matter, 
please contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
DMG:JA:ld 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel

. ,1 
School District in 
of the District 
the District 
for collec-
Sacramento County. 
Three of the seven members 
Board of Trustees have spouses who are employed by 
and are members of the certificated bargaining unit 
tive bargaining purposes. 
The District has requested us to draft an opinion on 
whether a board member may participate in a decision to ratify a 
collective bargaining agr~ment affecting certificated employees, 
including his or her own spouse. 
We have reviewed relevant 
provisions of the Fair Political Practices Act, regulations, and 
your advisories 8-83-227 and 8-85-122 on this SUbject. 
We understand that a board member does not 
disqualify himself or herself with regard to a decision 
have to 
related 
solely to the spouse I s salary, per diem, or reimbursement for 
expenses from the District (Government Code section 82030 (b) (2) 
and 2 Cal. Code of Regulations, section 18702.1(c) (2». 


LITTLER, MENDELSON, FASTIFF & TICHY 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
December 5, 1988 
Page 2 
Collective bargaining agreements for school district 
employees universally cover many aspects of "compensation" in 
addition to "salary, per diem, or reimbursement for expenses." 
Examples include health benefits, deferred compensation, 
tax 
sheltered annuities, life insurance, leaves, etc. 
Furthermore, 
the Board of Trustees has no means to isolate ratification of 
"salary" as opposed to "items of compensation" in discussing or 
ratifying a collective bargaining agreement. 
In light of this practical problem, we ask that the 
FPPC provide the District, 
~nd indirectly all school districts, 
with 
an 
advisory opinion 
on 
whether Government 
Code 
Section 82030(b) (2) and/or Title 2, California Code of Regula-
tions, Section 18702.1(c) (2) 
may be interpreted to authorize a 
board member to participate in a 
decision related solely to 
approval of a 
collective bargaining agreement for District 
employees which includes the "compensation" of a spouse as that 
term has been described herein. 
Without such a clarification, it would appear that the 
purpose of the above-cited section, regulation and prior advisory 
opinions by the FPPC, have no practical application to a govern-
mental entity which establishes employee "compensation" through 
collective bargaining. 
If you have any further questions, please do not 
hesitate to contact us.

Very truly yours,

..JOHN W. KYLE 
BENJAMIN W. HAHN 
We represent the River Delta Unified School District in 
Sacramento County. 
Three of the seven members of the District 
Board of Trustees have spouses who are employed by the District 
and are members of the certificated bargaining unit for collec-
tive bargaining purposes. 
The District has requested us to draft an opinion on 
whether a board member may participate in a decision to ratify a 
collective bargaining agreement affecting certificated employees, 
including his or her own spouse. 
We have reviewed relevant 
provisions of the Fair Political Practices Act, regulations, and 
your advisories 8-83-227 and 8-85-122 on this subject. 
We understand that a board member does not have to 
disqualify himself or herself with regard to a decision related 
solely to the spouse's salary, per diem, or reimbursement for 
expenses from the District (Government Code section 82030 (b) (2) 
and 2 Cal. Code of Regulations, section 18702.1(c) (2». 


LITTLER, MENDELSON, FASTIFF & TICHY 
Fair Political Practices commission 
December 5, 1988 
Page 2 
Collective bargaining agreements for school district 
employees universally cover many aspects of "compensation" in 
addition to "salary, per diem, or reimbursement for expenses." 
Examples include health benefits, deferred compensation, tax 
sheltered annuities, life insurance, leaves, etc. 
Furthermore, 
the Board of Trustees has no means to isolate ratification of 
"salary" as opposed to "items of compensation" in discussing or 
ratifying a collective bargaining agreement. 
In light of this practical problem, we ask that the 
FPPC provide the District, and indirectly all school districts, 
with an advisory opinion on whether Government 
Code 
section 82030(b) (2) and/or Title 2, California Code of Regula-
tions, Section 18702.1(c) (2) may be interpreted to authorize a 
board member to participate in a decision related solely to 
approval of a 
collective bargaining agreement for District 
employees which includes the "compensation" of a spouse as that 
term has been described herein. 
without such a clarification, it would appear that the 
purpose of the above-cited section, regulation and prior advisory 
opinions by the FPPC, have no practical application to a govern-
mental entity which establishes employee "compensation" through 
collective bargaining. 
If you have any further questions, please do not 
hesitate to contact us. 
TF/tp 
351E.487 
Very truly yours, 
OPHELIA H. ZEFF

Dear Mr. James: 
December 21, 1988 
Re: 
88-469 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on December 16, 1988 by the Fair Political 
Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact Jeevan Ahuja, an attorney in 
the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written 
advice. 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to information needed. 
If your request is for 
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 
18329) .) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
, 
DMG:plh 
Very truly yours, 
J)/:~/~.

sections.question:
The spouses of board members Paul E. Stefani, Milo Wetzel 
and Rosemarie Butler are employed by the River Delta Unified 
School District (the "District") and are members of the 
Y, et seq. 
All references to 
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code 
of Regulations. 
"8 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 


Victor J. James II 
January 13, 1989  
certificated bargaining unit for collective bargaining 
purposes. 
May the board members participate in a decision to 
ratify a collective bargaining agreement affecting the 
certificated employees including their spouses?
sections.conclusion:
Under the Act, the board members may participate in the 
decision to ratify the collective bargaining agreement 
affecting the certificated employees including their spouses.
sections.facts:
You have indicated that you understand, based on section 
82030(b) (2) and Regulation 18702.1(c) (2) (now renumbered as 
Regulation 18702.1(c) (1» 
that a board member does not have to 
disqualify himself or herself with regard to a decision solely 
related to a spouse's salary, per diem, or reimbursement for 
spouses from the District. 
You are concerned, however, since 
the collective bargaining agreement for school district 
employees covers many aspects of compensation. 
In addition to 
salary, the agreement includes health benefits, deferred 
compensation, tax sheltered annuities, life insurance, leaves, 
etc.
sections.analysis:
In the past the commission has held that pension benefits 
received from a county are not income within the meaning of 
Section 82030(a). 
In its opinion, the Commission noted: 
When an employee agrees to work for the county, 
he is entitled to certain benefits which comprise 
his compensation. 
He receives, for example, a 
monthly salary, vacation and sick leave and 
retirement benefits .... 
[A]s the California 
Supreme Court has observed, retirement benefits, 
such as pension, "do not represent the 
beneficient (sic) gratuities of the employer; 
they are, rather, part of the consideration 
earned by the employee." 
In other wordS, the 
pension payment serves as a renumeration for 
services rendered by the employee .... " 
A 
pension, therefore, is essentially a type-of 
deferred salary whereby an employee agrees to 
receive a smaller payment while working in 
exchange for the security of receiving the 
remaining portion of his compensation after he 
retires. 
In re Moore, (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 33, 36 
(citations and footnote omitted, 
emphasis added). 


Victor J. James II 
January 13, 1989  
The analysis in Moore, supra, is applicable to other fringe 
benefits as well such as health benefits, deferred 
compensation, tax sheltered annuities, life insurance, leaves, 
etc. 
These fringe benefits are part of salary in that the 
employee agrees to receive a smaller payment in exchange for 
receiving the remaining portion of his compensation by way of 
these fringe benefits. 
Thus, if such fringe benefits are part 
of a compensation package paid by a government agency, they are 
included within the term salary in section 82030(b) (2). 
Accordingly, the three members of the River Delta Unified 
School District Board of Trustees may participate in the 
decision to ratify a collective bargaining agreement affecting 
the certificated employees including their spouses. 
However, 
they may not participate in decisions to hire, fire, promote, 
demote, or discipline an official's spouse, or to set a salary 
for an official's spouse which is different from salaries paid 
to other employees of the spouse's agency in the same job 
classification or position. 
(Regulation 18702.1(c) (1), copy 
enclosed.) 
See also Saint-Evens Advice Letter, No. A-83-172, 
and Flitner Advice Letter, No. A-87-096 (copies enclosed). 
Although the Act may not require disqualification from a 
particular decision, other statutory provisions, such as 
Government Code section 1090, may restrict the board members 
ability to participate in the decision. 
The Commission, 
however, does not administer this section. 
We are, therefore, 
unable to give you any advice regarding Section 1090.

QUESTION: The spouses of board members Paul E. Stefani, Milo Wetzel 
and Rosemarie Butler are employed by the River Delta Unified 
School District (the "District") and are members of the 
Y, et seq. 
All references to 
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code 
of Regulations. 
"8 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 


Victor J. James II 
January 13, 1989  
certificated bargaining unit for collective bargaining 
purposes. 
May the board members participate in a decision to 
ratify a collective bargaining agreement affecting the 
certificated employees including their spouses?

CONCLUSION: Under the Act, the board members may participate in the 
decision to ratify the collective bargaining agreement 
affecting the certificated employees including their spouses.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission January 13, 1989 Victor J. James, II Littler, Mendelson, Fastiff & Tichy 455 Capitol Mall, suite 420 Sacramento, CA 95814-4405 Dear Mr. James: Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-88-469 You have requested advice on behalf of board members Paul E. Stefani, Milo Wetzel and Rosemarie Butler of the River Delta Unified School District Board of Trustees concerning their responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").Y This letter addresses only the duties of the school board members under the Act. There may be other restrictions imposed by other statutes, such as Government Code section 1090. The district attorney and the Attorney General are responsible for enforcing Section 1090. QUESTION The spouses of board members Paul E. Stefani, Milo Wetzel and Rosemarie Butler are employed by the River Delta Unified School District (the "District") and are members of the Y Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. "8 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 Victor J. James II January 13, 1989 Page 2 certificated bargaining unit for collective bargaining purposes. May the board members participate in a decision to ratify a collective bargaining agreement affecting the certificated employees including their spouses? CONCLUSION Under the Act, the board members may participate in the decision to ratify the collective bargaining agreement affecting the certificated employees including their spouses. FACTS You have indicated that you understand, based on section 82030(b) (2) and Regulation 18702.1(c) (2) (now renumbered as Regulation 18702.1(c) (1» that a board member does not have to disqualify himself or herself with regard to a decision solely related to a spouse's salary, per diem, or reimbursement for spouses from the District. You are concerned, however, since the collective bargaining agreement for school district employees covers many aspects of compensation. In addition to salary, the agreement includes health benefits, deferred compensation, tax sheltered annuities, life insurance, leaves, etc. ANALYSIS In the past the commission has held that pension benefits received from a county are not income within the meaning of Section 82030(a). In its opinion, the Commission noted: When an employee agrees to work for the county, he is entitled to certain benefits which comprise his compensation. He receives, for example, a monthly salary, vacation and sick leave and retirement benefits .... [A]s the California Supreme Court has observed, retirement benefits, such as pension, "do not represent the beneficient (sic) gratuities of the employer; they are, rather, part of the consideration earned by the employee." In other wordS, the pension payment serves as a renumeration for services rendered by the employee ....
